docs.title: Welcome to Yantra Launcher Wiki! 🚀
docs.text:
Hello there, CLI enthusiast! We're thrilled to have you here.

Yantra Launcher isn't just another Android launcher; it's a bridge between the classic efficiency of the command line and the modern versatility of your smartphone. Whether you're a seasoned terminal veteran or a curious newcomer looking to boost your productivity, you've come to the right place.

docs.title: What is Yantra Launcher?
docs.text: Yantra is a minimal, yet powerful, CLI-based launcher for Android. It's designed for those who find peace in the terminal and efficiency in a well-typed command. It strips away the clutter of icons and widgets, replacing them with a sleek, responsive interface where your keyboard is the primary tool.

docs.title: Why use Yantra?
docs.text:
• Blazing Fast: Launch apps, call contacts, or search the web in seconds—no more digging through app drawers.
  • Productivity First: Built-in tools for notes, todos, alarms, and calculators keep you in the flow.
  • Deeply Customizable: From themes and fonts to custom aliases and scripts, make the terminal truly yours.
  • Power User Ready: Execute Lua scripts, integrate with Termux, and automate your daily tasks with init scripts.

docs.title: How to navigate these docs?
docs.text:
We've organized this wiki to help you get the most out of your Yantra experience:

  • Quickstart: If you've just installed the app, start here to learn the basics.
  • Commands Overview: A high-level look at what Yantra Launcher's commands can do.
  • Commands Reference: Deep dives into every single command, from ai to weather.
  • FAQ: Common questions and troubleshooting tips.

docs.title: Join our Community 🤝
docs.text:
Yantra is more than just an app; it's a project fueled by the community. We'd love to hear your feedback, see your custom themes, and help you out if you get stuck.

  • Discord Server: Chat with the developer and other users in real-time.
  • Reddit Subreddit: Share your setups and find tips.
  • GitHub Repository: Report bugs, request features, or even contribute to the code!

"In the world of icons and swipes, be the one who types." ⌨️

Live the CLI Experience!

docs.title: Commands Overview
docs.text:
This page provides a quick overview of all commands in Yantra Launcher that you can glance. It does not try to explain every concept in detail. For getting a detailed perspective, see the individual command documentation.

Note

You may not see all these commands in the Minimal version of Yantra Launcher.

docs.title: launch [-p/-s] [app/package name/shortcut label]
docs.text: Launches specified app or shortcut. Example: 'launch Chrome'. Use the '-p' flag to launch app by package name. Example: 'launch com.android.chrome'. Use the '-s' flag to launch a Shortcut. Example: 'launch -s Chats'

docs.title: help | help [command_name]
docs.text: Documentation/Manual for all commands of Yantra Launcher. Use 'help cmd_name' to get documentation for specific command.

docs.title: community [discord|reddit]
docs.text: Redirects to the Discord server of Yantra Launcher. Here you can share and get Feedback, Suggestions, Insights, tips and CLI emotions from other users like you! Use the optional 'discord' or 'reddit' parameters to navigate to a specific platform.

docs.title:
theme

Applies specified theme to Yantra Launcher. Example: 'theme Tokyonight'

call [name | number]

Calls specified contact name. If contact name is not found then the raw input (considered as phone number) is called.

bluetooth [state]

Toggles bluetooth on/off. Example: 'bluetooth on' or 'bt 0'

flash [state]

Toggles flashlight on/off. Example: 'flash on' or 'flash 0'. Use without any args to toggle the state.

internet

Opens a panel containing settings to enable internet connection.

ai [message/reset]

A simple tool to access chatGPT from the terminal. Based on OpenAI's gpt-3.5-turbo, you can chat with your own AI assistant. An API key is required to be entered in 'settings'. When you want to erase the message history to save tokens, you can use the 'reset' message ('ai reset').

todo

A simple TODO utility. Use 'todo' to get list of tasks with their indexes and progress. Add a task like 'todo Go for a brisk walk'. Mark tasks as done using their index returned from 'todo' command, like 'todo 2' marks the 3rd task as done. Use 'todo -1' to clear list.

Use the -p argument to mark optional progress for any task, like 'todo -p 1 30' marks the 2nd task as 30% done (Syntax: todo -p taskIndex progress). Use 'todo -p -1' to reset progress for all tasks.

alias

Unix-like aliasing system to set short-hand commands for available commands. Note that Yantra Launcher saves all aliases in memory and is retained even after restarting the Launcher session. Pre-defined commands can not be aliased. Usage (to set or update an alias): 'alias alias_name = alias_cmd' Example: 'alias h = help' Use 'alias alias_name' to get value of the alias. Use 'alias' to get list of all current aliases. Use 'alias -1' to reset to default aliases.

weather

Fetches the weather report for specified location. Example w new delhi

username

Used to change username. Example: username johnDoe

cd [path]

Changes the current directory to the specified path. Example: 'cd DCIM'

ls [-a]

Lists contents in the current directory. Optionally, pass the '-a' flag to also show hidden files/folders.

open [file name]

Opens specified file. Example: 'open certificate.pdf'

search

Searches the internet for the provided query. Search engine can be specified with the -e flag (-e=google|duckduckgo|brave|bing|yahoo|ecosia|startpage|qwant|you|playstore). Default is google. You can use a custom search engine by specifying the url with the -u flag (-u=https://example.com/search?q=). The query is the only required argument that is provided at the end of the command. It is automatically URL encoded during execution of the command. Examples: 'search Yantra Launcher' 'search -e=duckduckgo Yantra Launcher' 'search -u=https://example.com/search?q= Yantra Launcher'

web

Opens the specified URL in your browser, if present, ofc!

GUPT | GUPT [url]

G.U.P.T stands for Get Undercover Private Tab. Tired of going to browser everytime and opening Incognito Tab. With GUPT command, you get a built-in Private incognito Tab. Example: 'gupt https://www.youtube.com'. Use without url to default to https://www.google.com

tts

Speaks provided text (Text-to-Speech). Example: 'tts Travel, World!'

news

Opens the news website configured via settings. Defaults to Google News

bored

Helps you find things to do when you're bored! (using Bored API)

time [utc] [GMT]

Shows current local Date and Time. Use the utc arg to get UTC time. An optional time difference parameter can add or subtract that from UTC time. Example: time time utc time utc +5:30

alarm [time] [message]

Sets alarm using a supported Alarm Clock on your device. Use without args to open the list of alarms on your device default app. The time must be in 24 hr format. It can optionally be followed by a message string to display in alarm. Examples: 'alarm 14:30' sets alarm for 2:30 pm 'alarm 0:15 Wish good night to ***' sets alarm for 12:15 am with a message to display.

timer [length] [message]

Used to create a countdown timer using the default app on your device. Use without args to launch the timer app. The length must be in seconds and ranges from 1 to 86400 (24 hrs). It can optionally be followed by a message string to display in timer. Examples: 'timer 60' sets timer for 1 minute 'timer 3600 Take out the trash' sets timer for 1 hour with a message to display.

sysinfo | sysinfo -component

Displays system information, much like 'Neofetch'

screentime [app-name][-all]

Shows Total Screen time for the day! Give app name to get screen time for particular app, or use the '-all' flag to get screentime for all apps used today. Example: 'screentime Instagram' or 'screentime -all'

scripts

Opens dialog for creating, modifying and deleting custom scripts for Yantra Launcher, to execute multiple commands at once. Scripts can have Yantra Launcher commands in it. But you can also create exclusive Lua scripts for extended functionality. If you are using Lua code, you can even call Yantra Launcher commands from the script using the binding.exec() function. Example: 'binding.exec(text Scripts are cool!)'. Also see the 'run' command.

quote

Displays a random quote! What else do you expect?

bg | bg random [-id=53] [-grayscale] [-blur=2]

'bg' is used to set custom Wallpaper from the Albums. Use 'bg -1' to remove custom Wallpaper and set to solid theme background. Use 'bg random' to fetch a random Wallpaper from the picsum.photos. You can fetch a specific image by passing its id as a flag parameter (full list at https://picsum.photos/images). An optional -grayscale flag is used to get a grayscale image. Get a blurred image by providing the blur flag with intensity from 1 to 10. Note that this command only changes the Home screen wallpaper, not the Lock screen one.

text

Broadcasts text message. Example: 'text Yantra is cool!'

translate <-language>

Translator based on Google Translate. Provide a valid language code flag and the text to translate. The source language is automatically detected. Example: translate -fr Hello

music [play/pause/prev/next]

Control the local music state on your device. Use without any parameters to toggle the music state. Use 'play' to resume music, 'pause' to pause music, 'next' to play the next track and 'prev' to play the previous track in the queue.

echo [-mode]

Prints specified text to the terminal with the given mode. Here mode is an optional argument (e: Error text, s: Success text, w: Warning text) representing the nature of the text output. If mode is not specified, the text is printed (normal)ly Examples: 'echo -e An error occurred.' 'echo Hello, World'

speedtest

Opens a small GUI speedtest utility to check your internet speed, powered by openspeedtest.com.

notify

Fire a notification with the given message. Example: notify Opened work apps

calc [expression]

An in-built calculator to evaluate arithmetic expressions. It does addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, exponentiation (using the ^ symbol), and a few basic functions like sqrt, sin, cos and tan. It supports grouping using (...), and it gets the operator precedence and associativity rules correct.

Example: 'calc ((4 - 2^3 + 1) * -sqrt(33+44)) / 2' gives 7.5

email

Launches email app with recipient set. Example: 'email coderGtm@gmail.com'

sleep

Pauses Yantra Launcher for specified milliseconds. Usage: 'sleep numOfMilliseconds' Example: 'sleep 5000'

vibe

Vibrates the device for specified duration (in milliseconds). Example: 'vibe 1000' does a 1 second vibration.

init

A special script (function) to execute specified commands automatically whenever Launcher is opened or navigated to.

launchf [approx app name]

Launches app by matching given app name string using fuzzy search algorithm (Levenshtein distance). Example: 'launchf tube' may launch YouTube.

info [-p] [app/package name]

Launches app settings page for specified app. Example: 'info Big Battery Display' or 'i Farty Orbit'. Use the optional '-p' flag to launch app settings by package name. Example: 'info com.android.chrome'

infof [approx app name]

Launches app settings by matching given app name string using fuzzy search algorithm (Levenshtein distance). Example: 'infof tube' may open system settings for YouTube.

uninstall

Uninstalls the specified app. Example: 'u Instagram'

unalias

Used to un-alias (remove) an alias. Usage: 'unalias alias_name' Example: 'unalias h' Use 'unalias -1' to remove all aliases

termux [args]

Runs given command in termux if it is installed and logs the stdout generated. Note that the 'allow-external-apps' property must be set to true in ~/.termux/termux.properties in Termux app and RUN_COMMAND permission must be given from Yantra Launcher's settings page. Also, the result is returned only after the command is fully executed (exited) due to API restrictions. Usage: termux cmd_name [args] Example: 'termux echo Hello from Termux' To resolve any issues still occurring, ask in Discord Community or mail me at coderGtm@gmail.com.

run script_name

Used to run your custom scripts. Use the '-lua' flag to run the script as Lua code. Normal scripts can be run with the '-clean' flag to not print the command logs while execution and just log the output of the commands.

backup [-i]

A utility to backup your Yantra Launcher configuration. Using the command will export your current configuration to a file. If you use the -i flag, you can import a configuration file that will replace your current configuration.

dict [-urban]

Search for the meaning of a word in an online dictionary (freeDictionaryAPI). Use the '-urban' flag to search in Urban Dictionary!

battery

Shows current Battery Level. Use the optional '-bar' argument to show just the battery percentage and charging status in visual form.

lock

Applies Screen Lock to your Yantra (device). Note: Appropriate permissions are required.

cmdrequest

In case you want any new commands to be added to Yantra Launcher, run this command to open the GitHub issue template for command request. Note that it is not guaranteed that the command request will be accepted but I'll try my best to see if I could accommodate it in the upcoming versions of Yantra Launcher, if suitable.

feedback

Please provide your valuable feedback and any features you want in the next release of the app. Consider giving a 5 star review on the Play Store if you like the app.

exit

Exit Launcher. Note: Launcher will restart if set as default Launcher
1. How to integrate Yantra Launcher with Termux?
docs.text:
Ensure that termux is installed on your device from github or f-droid. Follow the steps below to integrate Yantra Launcher with Termux:

  1. Open Yantra Launcher.

  2. Type any termux command to invoke the Permission Dialog. For example, enter

    termux ls

  3. Click on Allow in the permission dialog.

  4. Now, manually launch the termux app. You can do it via the following Yantra Launcher command:

    launch termux

  5. Set allow-external-apps property to true, by entering the following code in termux:

    value="true"; key="allow-external-apps"; file="/data/data/com.termux/files/home/.termux/termux.properties"; mkdir -p "$(dirname "$file")"; chmod 700 "$(dirname "$file")"; if ! grep -E '^'"$key"'=.*' $file &>/dev/null; then [[ -s "$file" && ! -z "$(tail -c 1 "$file")" ]] && newline=$'\n' || newline=""; echo "$newline$key=$value" >> "$file"; else sed -i'' -E 's/^'"$key"'=.*/'"$key=$value"'/' $file; fi

    Source: https://github.com/termux/termux-tasker?tab=readme-ov-file#allow-external-apps-property-optional

  6. Now, you can easily execute Termux commands from your Yantra Launcher terminal. If you get an error though saying something about Termux not running in background, follow the steps mention here: https://github.com/coderGtm/yantra-app-launcher/issues/5#issuecomment-1778961986

Feel free to open an issue or discuss in the Discord community if you still face any problems!

docs.title: 2. How to get the ai command working?
docs.text:
Getting ChatGPT like responses in your Home Screen Terminal is so cool that you don't wanna miss this feature. But to get it working, you need to follow some steps:

  1. Choose an AI Provider. It could be anything that conforms to the ChatGPT API Response style. You can even use a paid OpenAI API in Yantra Launcher. Free alternatives may be found here: https://github.com/zukixa/cool-ai-stuff.
  2. Get the base domain of the service. For example, Naga AI's API url is https://api.naga.ac/v1/chat/completions. So the base domain here would be "api.naga.ac". Enter this base domain only in the Yantra Launcher Settings by using the settings command.
  3. Next, get the API Key. This is the most important part. How you get an API key can differ from one service provider to other, so it's best to check out their specific instructions. For example, to get Naga AI's API Key, you need to join their Discord server and send a message there. For more assistance you may ask in their server or in Yantra Launcher's community server.
  4. Enter this API Key in Yantra Launcher's Settings.
  5. If you followed all the steps correctly and your service provider is working properly then you can now use the ai from your Yantra Launcher terminal.Test it by sending a message like:
ai Hello World

Feel free to discuss in the Discord community if you still face any issues!

docs.title: 3. What is the 'gupt' command?
docs.text:
G.U.P.T stands for "Get Undercover Private Tab". Tired of going to browser everytime and opening Incognito Tab. With GUPT command, you get a built-in Private incognito Tab. Example:

gupt https://www.youtube.com

Tip

Use without url to default to https://www.google.com.

Salient features of G.U.P.T:

  1. Launch a private browsing tab inside Yantra Launcher.
  2. All the data is cleared after closing the tab.
  3. You can also open a specific url in the private tab.
  4. Hidden from the recent apps list.
  5. No history is saved.
  6. No cookies are saved.
  7. No more going through the hassle of opening an incognito tab in your browser.

docs.title: 4. How to use Lua scripting in Yantra Launcher?
docs.text:
Lua Scripting is a very powerful feature addition to Yantra Launcher using which you can literally do almost anything within your launcher. The reason being obvious: Lua is an entire programming language which has been embedded in the Launcher. This FAQ assumes that you already know how normal Yantra Launcher scripts work. If you don't know then pleas read the documentation of that command using the help scripts command.

Creating a Lua script has the same process as creating a normal Yantra Launcher commands script:

  1. Enter the scripts command to open the scripts menu.
  2. Click on the button to create a new script and enter the script name.
  3. Enter the scripts command again to open the menu and click on your newly created script to open its editor.
  4. Enter the Lua code that you want to execute and click on the Save button.

As you saw, the script creation process is the same. But the difference lies in execution, namely the run command. To tell Yantra Launcher that you want to execute a script as Lua code, you need to pass the -lua flag to the run command. For example, suppose your script name is "jokeNotifier", you can run it using:

run -lua jokeNotifier

Also, note that the embedded Lua language may not have the modules you are loking for. This is because most modules are written in C while this embedding is based on Java (LuaJ). But worry not, I have included 2 custom modules for the embedding using which you can do amazing things. The first one is the http module to create and send HTTP requests and parse their responses. The second one is a binding module to execute Yantra Launcher commands from Lua scripts. Also, there are custom input and print functions to facilitate IO operations in the absence of an stdout. Here are some code snippets to show the usage:

-- A script to ask for a name and wait for 5 seconds before using a Yantra Launcher command
print("Enter your name:")
name = input()

-- Sleep for 5 seconds
os.execute("sleep 5")

-- Use Yantra Launcher's 'text' command to broadcast greetings
binding.exec("text Hello "..name.."!")

-- Script to notify a random cat fact

-- Make an HTTP GET request
local response = http.get("https://catfact.ninja/fact", {headers={}})

-- Use the 'exec' function of 'binding' to execute a Yantra Launcher command string
binding.exec("notify "..response.body.fact)

-- Example POST request
local postUrl = "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts"
local postData = '{"title": "foo", "body": "bar", "userId": 1}'
local headers = {
    ["Content-Type"] = "application/json"
}

-- Can use get, post, put, delete and patch
local response = http.post(postUrl, {body=postData, headers=headers})

-- Check for errors
if response.error then
    print("Error: " .. response.error)
else
    local body = response.body
    print("Response ID: " .. body.id)
    print("Title: " .. body.title)
    print("Body: " .. body.body)
    print("User ID: " .. body.userId)
end

I hope these examples are enough to get you started. If that's the case then probably you can now see the extent of power you have on your fingertips while using your Yantra Lancher.

(Yes, you know what I am saying...AUTOMATE)

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: This page aims to get you quickly started with Yantra Launcher on your Android device. It will be covering Installation and basic information about commands and their usage.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Yantra Launcher is available in 2 variants:

  1. Minimal
  2. Pro

The minimal version is for those who want the bare minimum set of commands and features. On the contrary, the Pro version has a wide array of useful commands and features. Both these variants are available to download from the Play Store. The Pro version is priced to support the project.

docs.title: Pre-built Official Versions (Direct Download)
docs.text:
Pre-built packages are recommended for most users as the app uses some Google Play Services (as much as I feel bad about it) which are ensured to be present on your device while downloading from the Play Store.

1) Minimal Version 2) Pro Version

docs.title: Building
docs.text: For building the app yourself, see here

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
Once you get the app, open it and tada... Welcome to Yantra Launcher! Your wallpaper will be gone and a solid black terminal background will be made available. Some text and a blinking cursor will be waiting for you below it!

The first thing you should (or rather must) do now is type help. Notice how the bottom suggestion bar changes as you type to make it easy for you. Hit enter and you will see a long text output. This is a short introduction to every command you can use in this terminal. Once you are done reading it, I am sure you can do basic tasks like launching apps.

docs.title: Setting as the Default Launcher
docs.text:
When you are somewhat comfortable with Yantra Launcher, you can set it as your default Launcher so every time you unlock your phone or exit an app, you go back to your Terminal Home Screen.

To do so, type settings and hit Enter. A GUI settings page for Yantra Launcher will open. Find the setting for selecting the default Launcher and click on it. It will show all the available Launchers on your device. Select Yantra Launcher and choose "Always". Yantra Launcher will now be your Home Screen. Enjoy!

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: To get a quick overview of all the commands, see the Commands Overview page. For detailed information on each command, check out the commands section in the sidebar.

docs.title: ai
docs.text: The ai command is used to access ChatGPT-like AI models from Yantra Launcher.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: ai [message/reset]

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
To use the ai command, you need 2 things:

  1. An AI provider, and
  2. An API Key
  3. (Optional) System prompt

An AI provider is a service that follows the OpenAI API Reference. Although you can use the ChatGPT API directly, keep in mind that it is a paid service.

Note

There are numerous free AI API providers that are compatible with the command as they follow the OpenAI standard. Some examples include NagaAI and Zukijourney.

The API Key is provided by the respective AI Service provider. The System Prompt is a type of instruction given to the AI model to follow for the entire conversation. Try playing with it to get interesting responses. Enter all these in the page opening by using the settings command.

Try out a simple message like 

ai hi
If a response is received from the AI model then you have successfully setup the command!

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
Now you can have a great conversation with your AI assistant right from your home screen! 

ai why should i use a command line launcher?
Use the ai reset command to clear the conversation history from Yantra Launcher

Warning

Keep in mind that the API you are using is charged by your provider based on the tokens used. The more you chat with ai, the more tokens are used as the entire conversation is being evaluated by the model. So, to keep usage minimum, consider using the ai reset command to clear the conversation history from Yantra Launcher when you no longer need the model to remember past conversations.

docs.title: alarm
docs.text: The alarm command sets an alarm using your device's default alarm app.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: alarm [time] [message]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• alarm: Opens the list of alarms in your default alarm app.
  • alarm <time>: Sets an alarm for the specified time (24-hour format, e.g., 14:30).
  • alarm <time> <message>: Sets an alarm with a custom message.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
alarm 18:30
alarm 07:00 Wake up!

docs.title: alias
docs.text:
Create short-hand commands for longer or frequently used commands.

Note

Yantra Launcher aliases are retained even after restarting the launcher session, unlike the aliases in usual or conventional shells (1).

  1. In this context usual or conventional shells refer to shells like Bash, Zsh, CMD, Powershell, etc. Yantra is unusual in this regard because of its unique features for aliases, which is persistency.

Note

Pre-defined commands can not be aliased.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
To set or update an alias: 

alias alias_name = alias_cmd

To get value of an alias: 

alias alias_name

To list all aliases: 

alias

To reset all aliases to default: 

alias -1

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Example IExample IIExample III

Simple Command Alias

alias h = help
This will create an alias h for the help command.

Alias With Arguments

alias ps = search -playstore
This will create an alias ps for the search -playstore command. This will let you search the Play Store with the ps command.

Example

ps Yantra Launcher

Alias for App Launch

alias cod = launch call of duty
This will create an alias cod for the launch call of duty command. This will let you launch Call of Duty with the cod command.

docs.title: backup
docs.text: The backup command is a utility to backup and restore your Yantra Launcher configuration.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: backup [-i]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• backup: Exports your current configuration (themes, aliases, scripts, settings, etc.) to a file.
  • backup -i: Imports a configuration file to restore settings.

Warning

Importing a backup will replace your current configuration and restart the launcher.

docs.title: battery
docs.text: The battery command displays the current battery level and status of your device.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: battery [-bar]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• battery: Shows detailed information including level, charging status, health, and temperature.
  • battery -bar: Shows the battery percentage and charging status in a visual progress bar format.

docs.title: bg
docs.text: The bg command is used to set a custom wallpaper or fetch random ones from the internet.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: bg | bg random [-id=53] [-grayscale] [-blur=2]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• bg: Opens the gallery to pick a custom image for the wallpaper.
  • bg -1: Removes the custom wallpaper and reverts to the solid theme background.
  • bg random: Fetches a random wallpaper from picsum.photos.
  • bg random -id=53: Fetches a specific image by its ID.
  • bg random -grayscale: Fetches a grayscale random image.
  • bg random -blur=5: Fetches a blurred random image (blur intensity from 1 to 10).

Note

This command only changes the Home screen wallpaper, not the Lock screen one.

docs.title: bluetooth
docs.text: The bluetooth command is used to toggle the Bluetooth state on your device.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: bluetooth [state]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• bluetooth on or bluetooth 1: Turns Bluetooth on.
  • bluetooth off or bluetooth 0: Turns Bluetooth off.

Note

On newer Android versions, the launcher might be restricted from toggling Bluetooth directly due to system security policies. In such cases, it will inform you about the restriction.

docs.title: bored
docs.text:
The bored command is a simple command that returns a random activity to do when you're bored. This command is powered by the Bored API.

Note

This command requires an active internet connection to work.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: bored

docs.title: calc
docs.text: The calc command is a built-in calculator for evaluating arithmetic expressions.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: calc [expression]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: The calculator supports: - Basic operations: +, -, *, /, ^ (exponentiation). - Functions: sqrt, sin, cos, tan. - Grouping: (...) for operator precedence.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
calc ((4 - 2^3 + 1) * -sqrt(3*3+4*4)) / 2
This expression evaluates to 7.5.

docs.title: call
docs.text:
The call command is used to make a phone call to any contact or phone number 📞.

Note

You will need to provide the Calling permission to Yantra Launcher in order to make calls. When you issue this command without the permission, Yantra Launcher will ask you for the permission via a system prompt.

Tip

In order to call a contact saved in your Contacts, Yantra Launcher needs access to read these contacts. You can quickly set this up by using the list contacts command which will ask you for the permission. Once granted, you should again use the command so that Yantra Launcher can read the list.

docs.title: Calling a saved contact
docs.text: You can quickly call any of your contact by just specifying the contact name after the call command.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
call Mumma

If multiple phone numbers exist for a given contact name, a popup dialog will be shown to select the number that you want to dial.

Tip

You can avoid the hassle of selecting a phone number that you frequently call from a popup by making an alias to directly call the specified phone number.

docs.title: Directly calling a phone number
docs.text: You can also directly call a raw phone number by providing it as the parameter to the call command.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
call 1234567890

Warning

If you provide a contact name and it is not in your contact list, Yantra Launcher will consider it as a raw phone number input and Android will parse it to call the parsed number.

docs.title: cd
docs.text: The cd command is used to change the current working directory in the terminal.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: cd [path]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• cd <path>: Changes the current directory to the specified path.

Note

This command is handled via a 3rd party open-source app called Croissant. You need to have Croissant installed and provide the necessary permissions for cd to work.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: cd DCIM

docs.title: clear
docs.text: The clear command is used to clear the terminal screen.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: clear

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Executing clear will remove all previous command outputs and text from the terminal, giving you a fresh, empty workspace.

docs.title: cmdrequest
docs.text: The cmdrequest command allows you to request new features or commands for Yantra Launcher.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: cmdrequest

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Executing cmdrequest will open a GitHub issue template specifically for command requests. While it doesn't guarantee the addition of the command, it's the best way to let the developer know what features you'd like to see in future versions.

docs.title: community
docs.text: The community command connects you with other Yantra Launcher users and the developer.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: community [discord/reddit]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• community: Opens the official Discord server for Yantra Launcher.
  • community discord: Navigates to the Discord server.
  • community reddit: Navigates to the Yantra Launcher subreddit.

Info

Join the community to get the latest updates, ask questions, share feedback, and discuss new features with other CLI enthusiasts!

docs.title: dict
docs.text: The dict command allows you to look up the meanings of words using an online dictionary.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: dict [-urban] <word>

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• dict <word>: Searches for the definition of the word using the Free Dictionary API.
  • dict -urban <word>: Searches for the word in the Urban Dictionary.

Note

This command requires an active internet connection.

docs.title: echo
docs.text: The echo command prints the specified text to the terminal.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: echo [-mode] <text>

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• echo <text>: Prints the text in the normal theme color.
  • echo -e <text>: Prints the text as an Error (usually red).
  • echo -s <text>: Prints the text as a Success message (usually green).
  • echo -w <text>: Prints the text as a Warning (usually yellow).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
echo -e An error occurred.
echo Hello, World

docs.title: email
docs.text: The email command launches your default email app with the recipient's address pre-filled.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: email <email-id>

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • email <email_address>: Opens the email client to compose a message to the specified address.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: email coderGtm@gmail.com

docs.title: exit
docs.text:
Exits Yantra Launcher. That's it!

Note

If you've set Yantra Launcher as the default Launcher, the system will restart the app. To prevent this, you'd need to set a different default launcher from the setting screen.

docs.title: feedback
docs.text: The feedback command allows you to share your thoughts, suggestions, or bug reports with the developer.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: feedback

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Executing feedback will open a dialog or provide information on how to send feedback via email (coderGtm@gmail.com) or the Play Store. Your input helps improve Yantra Launcher!

docs.title: flash
docs.text: The flash command toggles your device's flashlight.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: flash [on/off | 1/0]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• flash: Toggles the current state of the flashlight.
  • flash on or flash 1: Turns the flashlight on.
  • flash off or flash 0: Turns the flashlight off.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
flash on
flash

docs.title: gupt
docs.text: The gupt command (Get Undercover Private Tab) opens a built-in private incognito tab.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: gupt [url]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• gupt: Opens the private tab defaulting to Google.
  • gupt <url>: Opens the specified URL in the private tab.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: gupt https://www.youtube.com

docs.title: help
docs.text: As the name suggests, this command is your friend to navigate through the Launcher. It will provide you with a list of all available commands and their descriptions, with basic usage information.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
help
This command will display a list of all available commands and their descriptions.

If you want to know just about a specific command, you can use: 

help [command]

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
help
help launch
This will display detailed information about the launch command.

Tip

You can also long press any command suggestion to get a brief description of the command in the form of a popup dialog.

docs.title: history
docs.text: The history command displays a list of recently executed commands in the terminal.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: history

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • history: Lists the command history.

docs.title: info
docs.text: The info command launches the system settings page for a specified application.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: info [-p] [app/package name]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• info <app_name>: Locates the app by its display name and opens its settings.
  • info -p <package_name>: Opens the settings for the app with the specified package name.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
info Chrome
info -p com.android.chrome

docs.title: infof
docs.text: The infof command launches app settings using a fuzzy search algorithm.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: infof [approx app name]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • infof <string>: Matches the given string against installed app names using the Levenshtein distance algorithm and opens the settings for the closest match.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
infof tube
This might open the system settings for YouTube.

docs.title: init
docs.text: The init command opens a special script editor where you can define commands to be executed automatically every time the launcher is opened or navigated to.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: init

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• Type init to open the editor.
  • Enter one command per line.
  • Save the script.

Warning

This is a powerful feature. Avoid using commands like reset in the init script, as it can cause an infinite restart loop.

docs.title: internet
docs.text: Opens the system panel for toggling Internet connections. Can select Mobile Data, Wi-Fi, and even select the SIM or the Wi-Fi network to connect to.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: internet

docs.title: launch
docs.text: This is the most basic command and also the most used command in Yantra Launcher. As the name suggests, it is used to launch apps in different ways. Apart from apps, it can even launch app shortcuts.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: launch [-p/-s] app_name/package_name/shortcut_name

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
To launch any app by its name, you can start typing the name of the app and select it from the suggestions bar (if you have kept it enabled). Also, you can configure Yantra Launcher to directly launch an app if it is the last remaining option. See settings. 

launch whatsapp
You can also launch an app by its package name if needed, by providing the -p flag. This can be particularly useful when there are 2 apps with the exact same name and you don't want to face a selection popup everytime. Aliasing this command can be very useful in that case. 
launch -p com.xyz.gallery
Also, you can directly launch app shortcuts (the ones that you see when you long-press an app icon on traditional GUI app launchers) using the -s flag You can list all shortcuts using the list shortcuts command. For example, this command will launch the Spotify screen that shows songs tailored for you. 
launch -s Made for you

docs.title: launchf
docs.text: The launchf command stands for Fuzzy Launch. It uses a fuzzy search algorithm to find and launch the app that best matches your input.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: launchf [approx app name]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • launchf <string>: Searches for the app name that most closely matches the given string and launches it.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
launchf tube
This might launch the YouTube app if it's the closest match.

docs.title: list
docs.text: The list command displays information about various components in Yantra Launcher.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: list [apps/themes/contacts/shortcuts]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• list apps: Fetches and lists all installed applications.
  • list themes: Displays all available themes, including built-in and custom ones.
  • list contacts: Fetches and lists your contacts (requires contacts permission).
  • list shortcuts: Lists available app shortcuts.

docs.title: location
docs.text: The location command displays your current geographical information.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: location [-refresh]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• location: Shows address, coordinates (latitude, longitude), accuracy, and the source of the data.
  • location -refresh: Forces a fresh GPS update instead of using cached data.

Requirement

Location permissions must be granted to Yantra Launcher for this command to function.

docs.title: lock
docs.text: The lock command applies a screen lock to your device.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: lock

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
Executing the lock command will lock your device's screen.

Note

Appropriate permissions (Accessibility Service or Device Administrator) are required for this command to work. The launcher will guide you to enable these if they are missing.

docs.title: ls
docs.text: The ls command lists the contents of the current working directory.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: ls [-a]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• ls: Lists visible files and folders in the current directory.
  • ls -a: Lists all files and folders, including hidden ones.

Note

This command is handled via a 3rd party open-source app called Croissant. You need to have Croissant installed and provide the necessary permissions for ls to work.

docs.title: music
docs.text: The music command allows you to control local music playback on your device.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: music [play/pause/prev/next]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• music: Toggles the current music playback state (Play/Pause).
  • music play: Resumes music playback.
  • music pause: Pauses music playback.
  • music next: Skips to the next track in the queue.
  • music prev: Skips back to the previous track.

Info

This command controls the active media session on your device.

docs.title: news
docs.text: The news command opens the news website configured in your settings.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: news

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: By default, it opens Google News. You can change the news website URL in the Launcher settings.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: news

docs.title: notepad
docs.text: The notepad command is a simple in-app utility for taking quick notes.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: notepad [list / new <name> / read <name> / delete <name> / edit <name>]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• notepad list: Displays all your saved notes.
  • notepad new <note_name>: Creates a new note with the specified name.
  • notepad read <note_name>: Displays the content of an existing note.
  • notepad edit <note_name>: Opens the editor to modify a note's content.
  • notepad delete <note_name>: Deletes the specified note.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
notepad new TodoToday
notepad read TodoToday

docs.title: notify
docs.text: The notify command allows you to trigger a system notification with a custom message.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: notify <message>

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • notify <message>: Fires a notification displaying the provided text.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: notify Finished setting up the workspace

docs.title: open
docs.text: The open command is used to open a specified file using a compatible app on your device.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: open [file name]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • open <file_name>: Opens the specified file from the current directory.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: open certificate.pdf

docs.title: pro
docs.text: The pro command helps you upgrade to the Pro version of Yantra Launcher.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: pro

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Executing the pro command will redirect you to the Google Play Store page for Yantra Launcher Pro. The Pro version offers additional features and supports the development of the project.

docs.title: pwd
docs.text: The pwd command stands for Print Working Directory. It displays the absolute path of the current directory.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: pwd

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • pwd: Prints the current working directory path to the terminal.

docs.title: quote
docs.text: The command displays a random quote from a very huge collection of quotes pre-bundled with the app. It does not require an Internet connection.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: quote

docs.title: reset
docs.text: This command restart the Terminal Launcher completely. All temporary memory like command history is cleared as the app process is restarted.

docs.title: run
docs.text: The run command is used to execute custom scripts created within Yantra Launcher.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: run [-lua/-clean] script_name

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• run <script_name>: Executes the specified script.
  • run -lua <script_name>: Runs the script as Lua code.
  • run -clean <script_name>: Runs the script without logging the commands themselves, only their output.

Info

Scripts can be created and managed using the scripts command.

docs.title: screentime
docs.text: The screentime command displays the total time you've spent on your device today.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: screentime [app-name] [-all]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• screentime: Shows your total screen time for the current day.
  • screentime <app_name>: Shows the screen time for a specific application today.
  • screentime -all: Displays the screen time for all applications used today.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
screentime
screentime Instagram
screentime -all

Requirement

This command requires "Usage Access" permission. The launcher will prompt you to grant this permission if it's missing. Note that this feature requires at least Android 5.1.

docs.title: scripts
docs.text:
This is a command which allows you to create your own script on Lua language, or create the list of yantra's commands which will be executing command-by-command

Note

Currently, the scripts folder is internal to Yantra Launcher and cannot be accessed or edited directly using external text editors or file management applications.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
scripts

This command just open a dialog in which you can edit current scripts or create a new one.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Simple Lua code example:

print("Hello, World!")

A game: 

math.randomseed(os.time())
local secretNumber = math.random(1, 100)
local guess = nil
local attempts = 0

print("Guess the number between 1 and 100!")

while guess ~= secretNumber do
    print("Enter your guess: ")
    guess = tonumber(input())  -- You need to use input instead of io.read()
    attempts = attempts + 1

    if guess < secretNumber then
        print("Too low!")
    elseif guess > secretNumber then
        print("Too high!")
    else
        print("Congratulations! You guessed the number in " .. attempts .. " attempts.")
    end
end

Yantra command-by-command script 

termux python hs.py HELLO
launch discord
echo hi

docs.title: Notes about Lua:
docs.text: You need to use input instead of io.read() TO exe

docs.title: search
docs.text:
Query search engines then open the link on your default browser.

Tip

You can use alias feature to set your favorite engine as the default.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: search -e=<engine_name> -u=<URL>

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
-e (engine)-u (url)

Specify the search engine.

Default: google

Available Search Engines:

  • google
  • duckduckgo
  • brave
  • bing
  • yahoo
  • ecosia
  • startpage
  • qwant
  • you
  • playstore
  • maps

If your favorite search engine is not listed for -e flag, you can use this flag to provide a custom search engine to use.

Your search query will be inserted into the end of the URL.

Example:

- `-u=https://example.com/search?q=`

Info

Queries are URL-encoded before substitution.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Example IExample IIExample III Searching With Default Engine
search Yantra Launcher
Searching With Another Search Engine
search -e=duckduckgo Yantra Launcher
Using Custom Search Engines
search -u=https://example.com/search?q= Yantra Launcher

docs.title: setclr
docs.text: The setclr command allows you to change the terminal text color to a specific hex code, overriding the current theme.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: setclr <color>/-1

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• setclr <8_digit_hex_code>: Sets the terminal text color to the specified 8-digit hex code (without the #).
  • setclr -1: Resets the terminal text color to the theme's default color.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
setclr FF00FF00
setclr -1

docs.title: settings
docs.text: The settings command opens the main configuration panel for Yantra Launcher.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: settings

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Executing settings will launch a GUI where you can customize various aspects of the launcher, including configurations, behaviors, AI setup, and more.

docs.title: sfx
docs.text: The sfx command allows you to play sound effects directly from the terminal.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: sfx [sound_effect]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• sfx: Plays the default sound effect.
  • sfx <sound_effect_name>: Plays a specific sound effect that has been added to the internal storage via settings.

Tip

You can manage and add new sound effects in the Launcher settings.

docs.title: sleep
docs.text: The sleep command pauses the execution of commands for a specified amount of time.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: sleep [duration_in_ms]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • sleep <milliseconds>: Pauses the launcher for the specified duration.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
sleep 5000
This will pause the launcher for 5 seconds. This is particularly useful in scripts where you want to add a delay between two commands.

docs.title: speedtest
docs.text: The speedtest command opens a utility to check your current internet connection speed.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: speedtest

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Executing the speedtest command will launch a small GUI utility powered by OpenSpeedTest.com within the launcher to measure your download and upload speeds.

docs.title: support
docs.text: The support command provides information on how to support the development of Yantra Launcher.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: support

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Executing the support command will display a message about the project's open-source nature and redirect you to the Support section on the project's GitHub page. Supporting the developer helps in maintaining and adding new features to the launcher.

docs.title: sysinfo
docs.text: The sysinfo command displays detailed system information in a visual format, similar to the popular Neofetch tool on Linux.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: sysinfo | sysinfo -component

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• sysinfo: Displays a summary of your device's system information along with an ASCII art logo.
  • sysinfo -<component>: Can be used to fetch specific system details.

Tip

You can customize the ASCII art displayed by the sysinfo command in the Launcher settings.

docs.title: termux
docs.text: The termux command allows you to execute commands in the Termux environment and see the output in Yantra Launcher.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: termux <cmd> [args]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• termux <command>: Runs the specified command in Termux and logs the standard output (stdout).

Requirement

  1. Termux must be installed.
  2. allow-external-apps must be set to true in ~/.termux/termux.properties.
  3. RUN_COMMAND permission must be granted in Yantra Launcher settings.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: termux echo Hello from Termux

docs.title: text
docs.text: A simple command to send a text broadcast to the OS.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
text <message>
The system will broadcast the message to all the available messaging apps as well as apps that can handle text broadcasts.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
text Hi, how are you?
This will broadcast the message "Hi, how are you?" to the system which will then handle it accordingly.

docs.title: theme
docs.text: Used to set the theme for Yantra Launcher.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: theme <theme_name>

docs.title: Built-in Themes
docs.text:
There are many built-in themes in Yantra Launcher apart from the Default theme:

  1. Light
  2. Hacker
  3. Ocean
  4. Gruvbox
  5. Material
  6. Dark
  7. Solarized
  8. Dracula
  9. Monokai
  10. Green
  11. Red
  12. Blue
  13. Yellow
  14. Purple
  15. Orange
  16. Pink
  17. ubuntu
  18. Tokyonight
  19. Everforest
  20. Powershell

docs.title: Example
docs.text: theme Gruvbox

docs.title: Custom Theme
docs.text:
Apart from these built-in themes, you can even create your own custom theme using the "custom" parameter.

Creating a custom theme requires you to select the following parameters from a built-in color picker or enter the hex codes manually for them.

  • Background Color
  • Input Color
  • Command Color
  • Normal Text and Arrow Color
  • Error text Color
  • Positive text Color
  • Warning Text Color
  • Suggestions Color
  • Suggestion Bar Color

docs.title: Example
docs.text: theme Custom

docs.title: time
docs.text: The time command displays the current date and time.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: time [utc] [GMT]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• time: Shows the current local date and time.
  • time utc: Shows the current UTC time.
  • time utc +5:30: Shows UTC time with a specified offset.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
time
time utc +5:30

docs.title: timer
docs.text: The timer command is used to set a countdown timer using your device's default timer app.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: timer [length] [message]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• timer: Launches your default timer app.
  • timer <length>: Sets a timer for the specified duration in seconds (1 to 86400).
  • timer <length> <message>: Sets a timer with a custom message.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
timer 60
timer 3600 Take out the trash

docs.title: todo
docs.text: The todo command is a simple utility to manage your tasks.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: todo [task / index / -1 / -p]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• todo: Lists all your tasks with their indexes and progress.
  • todo <task>: Adds a new task to your list.
  • todo <index>: Marks the task at the specified index as completed.
  • todo -1: Clears your entire TODO list.
  • todo -p <index> <progress>: Marks a specific task as a percentage done (e.g., todo -p 0 50).
  • todo -p -1: Resets progress for all tasks.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
todo Buy groceries
todo 0
todo -p 1 75

docs.title: translate
docs.text: The translate command uses Google Translate to translate text from one language to another.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: translate <-language_code> <text>

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • translate -<lang_code> <text>: Translates the provided text into the language specified by the code. The source language is detected automatically.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
translate -fr Hello
translate -es How are you?

Tip

Use standard two-letter ISO language codes (like fr for French, es for Spanish, hi for Hindi, etc.).

docs.title: tts
docs.text: The tts command stands for Text To Speech. As the name suggests, it is used for converting a text input to an audio output.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
tts <text goes here>

Internally, it uses the Google Speech Synthesis engine for converting the text to audio. The engine is invoked for the period of the synthesis and then shut back down to preserve resources.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: tts Yantra Launcher is cool

docs.title: unalias
docs.text: The unalias command is used to remove an existing alias from your session.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: unalias alias_name

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• unalias <alias_name>: Removes the specified alias.
  • unalias -1: Removes all custom aliases and resets to default.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
unalias cod
This will remove the alias cod if it was previously set.

docs.title: uninstall
docs.text: The uninstall command allows you to remove applications from your device directly from the terminal.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: uninstall <app name>

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • uninstall <app_name>: Requests to uninstall the specified application. A system confirmation dialog will appear to confirm the removal.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: uninstall Instagram

docs.title: username
docs.text: This command is used to change the username of your terminal home screen. It is the name that is displayed at the start of the commands and the command input prompt.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: username <new_username>

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
username coderGtm
This sets the username to "coderGtm".

docs.title: vibe
docs.text: The vibe command makes your device vibrate for a specified duration.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: vibe [duration_in_ms]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: • vibe <milliseconds>: Vibrates the device for the given amount of time.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
vibe 1000
This will vibrate the device for 1 second.

docs.title: weather
docs.text: The weather command fetches and displays the current weather report and forecast for a specified location.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: weather <location> [-fields]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
• weather <location>: Displays a basic weather report for the given location.
  • weather list: Lists all available weather fields you can use to customize the output.
  • weather <location> -field1 -field2: Displays the weather for the location with specific fields (e.g., -temp, -humidity).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
weather London
weather "New York" -temp -humidity

Tip

Use weather list to see all supported fields like -uv, -wind, -feels, etc. If your location name has spaces, you can enter it directly or wrap it in quotes. All field arguments must come after the location.

docs.title: web
docs.text: The web command is used to open a web page in your default web browser.

docs.title: Example I
docs.text:
web https://reddit.com

Specifying the protocol is not necessary and the launcher will default to prefixing http:// in that case. Most websites will automatically reroute http traffic to https if it's available so you can safely ignore the protocol.

docs.title: Example II
docs.text:
web reddit.com
Note
